Issues.Body:
Quarantine `TestServerTests.LongPollingWorks` which fails intermittently with:

```

```

The error occurs in `DefaultHubDispatcher.OnDisconnectedAsync` when the DI service provider scope is disposed before the hub disconnect handler finishes — a timing-dependent race during long-polling connection teardown.

**Failures observed in multiple main branch builds.**

Issues.Title: [DO NOT MERGE] Diagnostic: overlay MSBuild Copy-delete logging + revert #66440
Issues.Body:
## ⚠️ TEMPORARY — DO NOT MERGE

Diagnostic PR to confirm the root cause of the intermittent **MSB3030 "Could not copy file ... because it was not found"** failures in parallel CI builds (dotnet/aspnetcore#62807, dotnet/msbuild#12927). **No audit/fs-trace logging** in this PR — binlog only.

## What this does

1. **Overlays a custom `Microsoft.Build.Tasks.Core.dll`** (from dotnet/msbuild#14122) over the SDK's copy after toolset init, in `eng/build.sh` and `eng/build.ps1` (prints SHA256 before/after the swap). The custom Copy task logs a single `Low`-importance (binlog-only) line right before it deletes an existing destination:

   `
   MSB-COPY-DELETE: deleting existing destination before overwrite: <path>
   `

2. **Reverts #66440** (the `_WaitForCopyLocalSourcesBeforeCopy` retry gate) so the underlying race can resurface and be captured.

## Why

The Copy task unlinks an existing destination before overwriting it. When parallel projects copy to/from a **shared output file**, that delete can briefly remove a file another project is reading as a Copy **source** → MSB3030. The delete is otherwise invisible in the binlog. This overlay makes it visible so the deleted path can be correlated with the MSB3030 source path.

## Version alignment

`global.json` SDK is `11.0.100-preview.6` which ships MSBuild `18.9.0-preview`; the overlay DLL is `18.9.0-dev` (msbuild main `e775a976`) — same `18.9.0` line, days of skew only.

- Overlay DLL SHA256: `0CBBBA602D76DD9CE0705BAEE8C180EC6C3BA2A79E6BA4B283760D82C0426E12`

## How to read results

After a CI run, in the build log look for `=== MSBuild Bootstrap Overlay` (confirms the swap), then in the binlog grep `MSB-COPY-DELETE`. If an `MSB3030` reproduces, correlate its source path with a `MSB-COPY-DELETE` of the same path from a parallel project.

Related: dotnet/msbuild#12927, dotnet/msbuild#14122, dotnet/aspnetcore#62807

Issues.Title: Fix test-quarantine unquarantine matching and re-quarantine detection
Issues.Body:
## Problem

The `test-quarantine` workflow run [27903471243](https://github.com/dotnet/aspnetcore/actions/runs/27903471243) produced two classes of incorrect output, each confirmed against live AzDO/Helix pipeline data.

### 1. Bad unquarantines — matched by leaf method name

The agent matched tests by bare leaf method name instead of fully-qualified name, so it borrowed an unrelated test's pass statistics:

- **#67355** unquarantined `HelloWorldTests.HelloWorld` using pass-stats from the unrelated `StartupTests.HelloWorld` (the target test had **zero** run evidence in the cited pipeline).
- **#67354** unquarantined the subclass override `ServerExecutionTests.ServerRoutingTest.NavigationLock_...` using the **passing base class** `Tests.RoutingTest.NavigationLock_...`, even though the actual quarantined subclass was **failing on main** (builds 1473858, 1473100).

Both PRs were closed.

### 2. Missed re-quarantine — `LongPollingWorks` treated as new

`TestServerTests.LongPollingWorks` was re-quarantined as a brand-new **Case A** quarantine (new issue #67348 via #67349) instead of a **Case B** re-quarantine reusing the original tracking issue [#65702](https://github.com/dotnet/aspnetcore/issues/65702), because:

- Case B only triggered within a **14-day window**, but this test was unquarantined 62 days earlier (#66367), so it fell through to Case A; and
- even in Case B, the agent searched for a `"Quarantine ..."`-titled issue, but #65702 is titled/labeled **`Known Build Error`**, so a title search missed it.

## Fixes (all in `test-quarantine.md`)

**Unquarantine matching:**
- Group AzDO rows by the exact, full `automatedTestName`.
- Compute pass rate and require **real passing-run evidence** only from rows whose fully-qualified identity (namespace + *concrete* declaring class + method) matches the `[QuarantinedTest]` attribute in source; **fail closed on zero rows**.
- Explicit handling for subclass overrides, inherited base methods, parameterized/theory rows, and the IIS multi-assembly prefix exception.

**Re-quarantine (Case B):**
- Detect previously-unquarantined tests **per-test, not per-file** — walk the file's `QuarantinedTest` history newest→oldest and stop at the most recent commit changing *this exact test's* attribute (a single file holds many tests).
- Removed the 14-day window (no time limit).
- **Evaluate Case B before Case A**, with an explicit "does not match Case B" gate on Case A.
- Derive the original tracking issue from the issue URL **removed by the unquarantine commit** — reusable regardless of label (`test-failure` or `Known Build Error`), open or closed.
- Fall back to Case A when no valid numeric issue URL is present.

The diff was reviewed by two independent models to convergence (both APPROVE).

Recompiled `test-quarantine.lock.yml` with `gh aw` v0.79.8.

Issues.Body:
## Failing Test

`Microsoft.AspNetCore.Components.E2ETest.ServerExecutionTests.VirtualizationCspTest.Virtualize_WithItems_DoesNotViolate_StrictStyleCspPolicy`

## Error Message

The test failed in CI (components-e2e pipeline 87) as an E2E Selenium test. The specific error details were not captured in the test result data, but the failure occurred 1 time out of 30 builds monitored (29 passing, 1 failing).

## Build

[Build 1472771](https://dev.azure.com/dnceng-public/public/_build/results?buildId=1472771&view=results) (pipeline 87)

## Notes

The test verifies that the Blazor `Virtualize` component with `Items` does not emit inline styles that violate a strict CSP `style-src` policy. This E2E test uses Selenium to navigate to the page and check the browser's security violation log. Flaky failures in CSP tests can occur due to browser timing, cache state, or transient rendering differences in CI.

Issues.Title: Fix test-quarantine activation crash from oversized part1_data env var
Issues.Body:
## Problem

The daily `test-quarantine` workflow's `activation` job has been failing (e.g. [run 27902364302](https://github.com/dotnet/aspnetcore/actions/runs/27902364302)) at the **Create prompt with built-in context** step with:

```
An error occurred trying to start process '/usr/bin/bash' with working directory '...'. Argument list too long
```

### Root cause

The `pre_activation` step assembles ~225 KB of CI failure data (`part1_data`) and exposes it as a single job output. gh-aw turns the prompt's `${{ needs.pre_activation.outputs.part1_data }}` reference into a single **environment variable** declared on the three prompt-building steps. Linux caps one env-var string at `MAX_ARG_STRLEN` (`32 * PAGE_SIZE` = 131072 bytes) during `execve` — so a >128 KiB value makes the step's `bash` fail to start, even though the step never actually uses the variable.

This was latent until #67322 fixed token scrubbing. Before that, GitHub's secret detector was skipping the entire `part1_data` output (`Skip output 'part1_data' since it may contain secret`), so the env var resolved **empty** and never approached the limit. With scrubbing fixed, the data now flows at full size and trips `MAX_ARG_STRLEN`.

## Fix

Split `part1_data` into **16 fixed, byte-bounded chunks** (≤ 80000 bytes each, well under 131072), one per `pre_activation` output (`part1_data_0..15`). The prompt concatenates the 16 placeholders **adjacently** so gh-aw's exact-string (`split().join()`) substitution reconstructs the JSON verbatim. Key properties:

- **No single env var exceeds the per-string limit**, and empty slots cost nothing, so total environment size is unchanged (~225 KB, same as before — total `ARG_MAX` was never the issue).
- Chunks split on **UTF-8 character boundaries**; a loud `FATAL` guards overflow rather than emitting truncated JSON (unreachable in practice — `json.dumps` emits ASCII, so 16×80 KB comfortably covers the existing `SAFE_OUTPUT` cap).
- Keeps #67322's scrubbing intact.

Only `.github/workflows/test-quarantine.md` was hand-edited; `test-quarantine.lock.yml` was regenerated with `gh aw` v0.79.8.

## Validation

- `gh aw compile` → 0 errors / 0 warnings.
- Chunk split/reassembly verified to round-trip exactly (ASCII and multibyte).
- gh-aw substitution confirmed to use exact-string `split().join()` (no greedy-regex / prefix collisions between `part1_data_1` and `part1_data_10`).
- Reviewed by two additional models; no blocking issues.

Issues.Title: Reshape BrowserConfiguration API per review (BrowserOptions) while preserving the JS wire format
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open PR "Reshape BrowserConfiguration API per review (BrowserOptions) while preserving the JS wire format" (#67337)

Issues.Title: API review feedback: BrowserConfiguration shape/placement (#66393)
Issues.Body:
Review feedback on the public API proposed in #66393 (`BrowserConfiguration` for server-to-client configuration). Overall the feature is welcome and justified — Blazor apps commonly need server-authored browser boot options across render modes, and the current mechanisms are fragmented. The notes below are shape/placement suggestions; the endpoint-builder entry point is already in good shape.

## Need / scenarios

- **Commonality:** happy-path; **audience:** end-app developer.
- **Environments:** flows wherever the server emits the boot document — static SSR/prerendered, Interactive Server, Interactive WebAssembly hosted by ASP.NET Core, and Auto. It is inert for purely client-side standalone WebAssembly with no server-rendered document — worth documenting.
- Inputs naturally come from `IHostEnvironment`, logging options, request culture, and endpoint/middleware configuration.

## Recommended shape changes

### `BrowserConfiguration` (the root model)
- Rename to **`BrowserOptions`** — it's an options bag; the `Options` suffix matches the rest of ASP.NET Core and avoids confusion with `IConfiguration`.
- **Seal** it (options/DTO types are sealed by default; can unseal later).
- `LogLevel`: use **`Microsoft.Extensions.Logging.LogLevel?`** instead of `int?` — the usage example passes a magic integer (`LogLevel = 2 // Warning`); a strongly-typed enum prevents that.
- Make the nested `Server`/`Ssr`/`WebAssembly` properties **non-null and initialized** (get-only). The proposed usage `config.Server.ReconnectionMaxRetries = 10` NREs unless each sub-object is pre-allocated — a pit-of-failure for the common path.

### `ServerBrowserOptions`
- Consider renaming to **`InteractiveServerBrowserOptions`** — here "Server" means *interactive server rendering*, not static SSR or the physical server; aligning with Blazor render-mode vocabulary avoids ambiguity.
- Seal it.
- `ReconnectionRetryIntervalMilliseconds` → **`ReconnectionRetryInterval` of type `TimeSpan?`**. Public .NET options express durations as `TimeSpan`; the serialized browser format can still use milliseconds internally.

### `SsrBrowserOptions`
- Seal it.
- `CircuitInactivityTimeoutMs` → **`CircuitInactivityTimeout` (`TimeSpan?`)**.
- `DisableDomPreservation` → positive **`PreserveDom`** (public options prefer positive `Enable*`/`Allow*`/`Preserve*` over `Disable*`; nullable still distinguishes "unset").

### `WebAssemblyBrowserOptions`
- Seal it.
- `EnvironmentVariables`: expose as a **non-null, initialized `IDictionary<string, string>`** (get-only) rather than a settable concrete `Dictionary<,>` — avoids handing out a concrete type and avoids a null collection on the common path.

### `ConfigureBrowser` (component)
- Seal it.
- Add **`[Parameter]`** (and **`[EditorRequired]`** on the required options) — component parameters need their attributes visible in the API shape and bound by the runtime.
- Rename `Configuration` → **`Options`** for consistency with the renamed model.
- Model `HttpContext` as a **`[CascadingParameter]`** (infrastructure that's absent once execution moves to the browser), not a normal settable parameter.

### `BrowserConfigurationHttpContextExtensions` (the `HttpContext` accessor)
- Move it **out of `Microsoft.AspNetCore.Http`** into the Blazor feature namespace, and rename to match the options type (`GetBrowserOptions`).
- **Why (grounded in the shipped API):** feature-specific `HttpContext` extensions live in the *feature's* namespace, not the broad `Microsoft.AspNetCore.Http` one — of the 27 `this HttpContext` extension methods in the shipped public API, only 3 sit in `Microsoft.AspNetCore.Http` (core concepts like `GetEndpoint`/server variables); 20 are in `Microsoft.AspNetCore.Authentication`, 2 in `Microsoft.AspNetCore.Routing`, and the closest Blazor precedent — `AcceptsInteractiveRouting` — is in `Microsoft.AspNetCore.Components.Routing`. Placing this helper in the broad HTTP namespace would surface it on every `HttpContext` via IntelliSense.

## What's already right

- `WithBrowserConfiguration(this RazorComponentsEndpointConventionBuilder, Action<…>)` is the correct entry point: it's an endpoint-builder extension, so `Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder` is the right namespace and returning the same builder follows the `With*` convention. (Only a consistency rename to track the options type — e.g. `WithBrowserOptions`/`configureOptions` — would be needed.)

## Note on the serialized format

The `<!--Blazor-Configuration:{}-->` payload is effectively a wire format; consider treating its schema as versioned/internal so future changes aren't breaking, as called out in the proposal's Risks.

---

Issues.Title: Add review-public-api Copilot skill
Issues.Body:
## Summary

Adds a knowledge-driven Copilot **skill** under `.github/skills/review-public-api/` that reviews a proposed public API the way the `@dotnet/aspnet-api-review` team would: it judges **whether the API should exist at all** (modeling scenarios, environments, common-vs-rare usage, input provenance), then proposes concrete **shape changes** — each with grounded reasoning — and a **ref-assembly diff against the existing surface**. It also encodes the **modern-C# "negative space"** — the language features the public surface deliberately avoids (public `record`/`record struct`) versus the ones it adopts (`required`/`init` over constructor-overload proliferation). Documentation/markdown only; no product code.

## How it was built

Mined from the repo itself, three sources:

1. **API-review decisions** — **449 decided issues** (api-approved / api-needs-work / declined) with all comments (the conventions doc is a stub; the rules live in the threads). A disjoint slice was held out for evaluation.
2. **The shipped public-API surface** — all **172 `PublicAPI.Shipped.txt` files (~24k members)** plus representative implementations, for quantified cross-cutting conventions (Task-over-ValueTask + `Async` suffix; `CancellationToken` last + default; read-only returns; the type-suffix taxonomy; the **method-family contract** — `Add*` registers services in `Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection`, `Use*`/`Map*` in `Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder`, regardless of the feature namespace).
3. **A type↔assembly study** — **151 assemblies / 2,956 types** + `.csproj` dependency & packaging metadata + source-confirmed type kinds and a base/implements graph, to derive **which kind of type belongs in which kind of assembly** (interfaces ≈46% of `*.Abstractions` vs ≈13% of implementations; abstractions are dependency *sinks*), the abstraction↔implementation and **provider three-layer** patterns, namespace-vs-assembly decoupling, **shared-framework vs standalone-NuGet** packaging, and **type-relationship** conventions (`Default<X> : X`; provider options/handler inheritance chains; results implementing `IResult` + granular metadata interfaces; behavioral attributes implementing framework interfaces; reuse-an-existing-extension-point; companion-type clusters).
4. **A modern-C# language-feature study** — the canonical "what's new in C#" feature set (C# 8→latest) cross-checked against the shipped public surface to separate genuine **negative space** from adoption-curve scarcity. Conclusion: public `record`/`record struct` is the one feature deliberately avoided (its synthesized equality/`with`/`Deconstruct`/copy-ctor freeze into the binary + ref-assembly contract and can't be evolved), while `required`/`init` are *recommended* over constructor-overload proliferation, and `static abstract`/default-interface-methods are allowed with concrete justification. Implementation-style idioms invisible at the API boundary are out of scope.

## Validation

- **Static check (skill-validator):** passes.
- **Held-out A/B evaluation (5 runs, statistical):** over a complex held-out scenario set (open `api-ready-for-review` proposals never used to derive the rules + decided issues with known outcomes), the skill **measurably improves** review quality where the unaided agent is weak — aggregate **+20.3%**, statistically significant (95% CI excludes zero), low overfit. The baseline often fails to review at all.
- **Real-proposal hardening:** iterated against live `api-ready-for-review` issues (#66830, #66391, #66393) — never block when the repo isn't at hand; require a grounded **Why**; show the scenario model; model input provenance.

## Grounding report

# Grounding report — `review-public-api` skill

This explains *why* the skill says what it says, and how confident we are that it reflects real
ASP.NET Core API-review practice. The auditable backbone — every source, finding, inference, and
decision — is in [grounding-evidence.md](grounding-evidence.md); this narrative cites its `E#`/`I#`/`D#`
ids inline.

## The gap

ASP.NET Core's public-API conventions are largely **unwritten**. The repo has a process doc
(`docs/APIReviewProcess.md`, E1–E2) but its conventions doc, `docs/APIReviewPrinciples.md`, is a
**stub** — literally "Principle 1 / Convention 1" (E3). An unaided agent asked to review a proposed API
therefore falls back on generic .NET design guidance and misses the repo-specific decisions the
`@dotnet/aspnet-api-review` team actually makes — especially the *first* question they ask, "should
this API exist at all?", and the recurring shape fixes (sealing, `CancellationToken`, naming the
mechanism, binary-compat). The skill exists to encode that tacit knowledge.

## What we investigated

The conventions live in the decision threads, so we mined them. We harvested **449 decided API-review
issues** — every `api-approved` (363), `api-needs-work` (30), and declined `api-suggestion` (40), plus
the open `api-ready-for-review` queue — with **all 3,789 comments** (the discussion, not just the
verdict). To measure generalization rather than recall, we held out ~18% of decided issues plus the
entire open queue as a validation set and never used them to write rules (D4). Rules were distilled
from the training split by parallel passes over the threads, each rule tied to the issue(s) and a
reviewer quote that evidences it. The baseline authority is the .NET Framework Design Guidelines (E5);
this skill is the ASP.NET-Core-specific layer on top.

We then corroborated the rules against what the repo **actually ships**, mining the public-API surface
itself — all 172 `PublicAPI.Shipped.txt` files (~24k members) — for quantified, cross-cutting
conventions (E36–E42): `Task`-over-`ValueTask` (≈82/18) with an `Async` suffix; `CancellationToken`
last + `= default`; read-only collection/dictionary returns; the type-suffix taxonomy; and the
method-family contract — `Add*` registers services in `Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection`,
`Use*`/`Map*` add middleware/endpoints in `Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder` regardless of the feature's
own namespace — confirmed by reading the implementations, not just the signatures (E42).

We extended this into a **type↔assembly** study (E43–E50): across the 151 public assemblies (2,956
types) plus `.csproj` dependency/packaging metadata and source-confirmed type kinds, we derived which
*kind* of type belongs in which *kind* of assembly (interfaces ≈46% of `*.Abstractions` vs ≈13% of
implementations; abstractions are dependency *sinks*), the abstraction↔implementation and provider
three-layer patterns, the namespace-vs-assembly decoupling, shared-framework vs standalone-NuGet
packaging, and the **type-relationship** conventions (`Default<X> : X`, provider options/handler
inheritance chains, results implementing `IResult` + granular metadata interfaces, behavioral
attributes implementing framework interfaces, reuse-an-existing-extension-point). The full analysis is
in the session's `ANALYSIS.md`; the confirmed rules are folded into the skill's conventions reference.

## What we concluded

The strongest, most repeated signal is **need justification** — reviewers decline or rework APIs that
lack demonstrated demand, that exist only for generic extensibility, that would encourage anti-patterns,
that pollute `HttpContext`/`WebApplication` IntelliSense, or that fuse separate concerns (E10–E13, E28).
So the skill makes a **need assessment** (commonality + audience precedence) a mandatory, first-class
part of every review (decision D3), not an afterthought to shape.

The shape rules are likewise grounded in concrete decisions: naming the *mechanism* and avoiding
implementation words (E14–E15); `CancellationToken = default` on new async methods and `Task`-over-
`ValueTask` for callbacks (E16–E17); collection/return-type choices and named result structs (E18, E26);
**seal-by-default** with marker/feature interfaces for real extensibility (E19–E20); binary-compat via
new overloads and restrained obsoletion, with breaking default changes gated on a major version + an
escape hatch (E21–E22); and consistency/placement with sibling APIs and namespaces (E23–E24, E29, E25).

Where the skill **generalizes beyond what any single thread states**, we flagged it: the requirement
that the review *locate the current public type in source and diff against it* (I1) is an inference, not
a quoted rule — it follows from the review being done in ref-assembly form (E2) combined with the fact
that this repo has **no checked-in ref source** (E7, ref assemblies are generated at build).

## Roads not taken

- **Corpus scope (D1).** We mined the *entire* decided corpus rather than a recent window, so durable
  long-standing conventions aren't lost; we discarded only the early, never-decided `api-suggestion`
  issues as low-signal.
- **Output format (D2).** We chose per-type **bullet changes + a `diff` against the existing repo API**
  over freeform prose, because prose hides the concrete delta — and explicitly diff against the *current*
  surface, not the submitter's proposal, per the requested contract.
- **Need vs shape (D3).** The skill judges whether to add the API at all, not just its shape, because
  that is the most common real decline reason.
- **Train/validation split (D4).** We hold out issues we never tuned against, so the eval measures
  whether the agent learned the *rules*, not the examples.

## What we refined empirically

The skill was then hardened against real proposals (the open `api-ready-for-review` queue), each pass
fixing a concrete failure mode: it must **reconstruct the existing surface from knowledge, not block**
when the repo isn't at hand; every recommendation needs a **grounded Why in prose** (this exposed and
removed a fabricated "auto-generate a cache key" suggestion that would have defeated the proposal's
bookmarkable-URL goal); the need assessment must **show its scenario model** — runtime environments
(flagging where the API is inert, e.g. static SSR) and common-vs-rare usage; and the model must include
the **provenance of inputs** — where the consumed data comes from in real apps (blob storage, DBs,
files) and what natural identifiers/metadata exist there (decisions D5–D8). The last one corrected a
review to keep a "required" `cacheKey` (callers usually already hold a natural id) instead of
synthesizing one expensively.

## How grounded it is

The claim-coverage lint over the skill's normative claims reports **29 claims, all grounded, 0 ungrounded** (see the appendix). Each maps to ≥1 real issue-backed evidence row (or, for the
diff-against-source workflow step, to inference I1 which itself resolves to evidence). The held-out
A/B evaluation (`skill-validator evaluate`, 5 runs) measures *that* the skill helps on issues it never
saw — scenarios drawn from #32443, #58110, #42467, #41946, #55656 (E30–E34); results are summarized in
the PR. This report shows *why* the guidance is trustworthy; the eval shows *that* it works.


---

**Coverage:** 29 claims, 29 cited, 0 inferred, **0 ungrounded**.

## Sources & findings

Proposals must be in ref-assembly format. Changes during impl restart the review. |

The skill must locate the public type in src/**/src/ to represent the CURRENT signature for the diff. |

Security ops require explicit opt-in. |

Extension-method classes end in Extensions and are static (371 types, 1447 extension methods). |

Core: 44% class + 21% interface + 12% attribute (shared impl + contracts). Implementation: 39% class + 17% static/extension + 13% sealed concrete (concrete-heavy). (No type-forwarding seen in shipped surface.) |

Optional/integration features that pull a third-party dependency ship as standalone NuGet (IsPackable/IsShipping): Caching.SqlServer (Microsoft.Data.SqlClient), Caching.StackExchangeRedis (StackExchange.Redis), Http.Polly (Polly), Logging.AzureAppServices. Rule: foundational -> shared framework; optional/integration/extra-dependency -> out-of-band package. |
| E49 | source | `src/** type declarations (base/implements graph, 1784 edges)` (repo_file) | What type-to-type relationship conventions recur? | Default<X>:X for framework default impls (17 pairs: DefaultHttpContext:HttpContext, DefaultProblemDetailsFactory:ProblemDetailsFactory) — strong. X:IX naming pair for obvious impls (136: UrlHelperFactory:IUrlHelperFactory) — softer. Provider options/handlers derive a shared family base (JwtBearerOptions:AuthenticationSchemeOptions; JwtBearerHandler:AuthenticationHandler<JwtBearerOptions>; OAuthOptions:RemoteAuthenticationOptions). *EventArgs:EventArgs. |
| E50 | source | `src/** implements graph (IResult 48, IEndpointMetadataProvider 30, etc.)` (repo_file) | How do types plug into framework extension points? | Result types implement IResult + granular metadata interfaces (IStatusCodeHttpResult 30, IValueHttpResult 26, IContentTypeHttpResult 11) and IEndpointMetadataProvider (30) to self-describe to OpenAPI; MVC uses IActionResult separately. Behavioral attributes implement the framework interface carrying the behavior (IFilterFactory 11, IOrderedFilter 16, IBindingSourceMetadata, IRouteTemplateProvider). Reuse the narrowest existing extension-point interface (IRouteConstraint 22, IModelBinder 17, IComponent 19) before inventing one. Features ship companion clusters (Options/Events/Context/Builder/Result/Metadata/Defaults) — coherence check, not mandatory. |

## Inferences

| ID | Statement | Derived from | Confidence | Rationale |
|----|-----------|--------------|------------|-----------|
| I1 | Skill workflow must include a step to locate the current public type(s) in source and reconstruct their existing signature before diffing to the recommended shape | E7,E2 | high | Output contract requires diff vs existing repo API, but no ref source is committed; agent reconstructs from src. |

## Decisions

### D1: Corpus scope for rule distillation
- **Decision:** Mine the entire decided corpus (approved+needs-work+declined), 449 issues
- **Options considered:** [{"opt":"recent window (.NET 8-11)","cons":"misses durable older conventions"},{"opt":"entire decided corpus","pros":"complete; user asked to mine all"}]
- **Rejected because:** A recent-only window would miss long-standing conventions still enforced
- **Discarded:** api-suggestion issues that never reached a decision (early/undecided) — low signal

### D2: Output format for the review
- **Decision:** Per type/file: bullet change list + ```diff``` fence existing->recommended
- **Options considered:** [{"opt":"freeform prose"},{"opt":"per-type bullets + diff vs EXISTING repo API","pros":"actionable, matches ref-assembly review"}]
- **Rejected because:** Freeform prose is not actionable and hides the concrete shape delta
- **Discarded:** Diffing against the submitter's proposal instead of the existing repo surface — user explicitly wants vs existing

### D3: Whether the skill judges need, not just shape
- **Decision:** Always include a need assessment: commonality + audience precedence
- **Options considered:** [{"opt":"shape-only"},{"opt":"also judge whether to add at all","pros":"matches real review first question"}]
- **Rejected because:** Shape-only would miss the most common real decline reasons
- **Discarded:** —

### D4: Train/validation partition
- **Decision:** Hold out ~18% decided + the open ready-for-review as validation
- **Options considered:** [{"opt":"distill from all, eval on same","cons":"data leakage"},{"opt":"hold out 18% decided + all open","pros":"measures generalization"}]
- **Rejected because:** Evaluating on training issues measures recall not generalization
- **Discarded:** —

### D5: Should each recommendation include reasoning?
- **Decision:** Require a Why paragraph (grounded prose) per type; drop any recommendation that cannot be justified; never suggest something that defeats a stated goal of the proposal
- **Options considered:** [{"opt":"bullets + diff only","cons":"agent invented ungrounded advice (auto-generate prefix) that defeated the proposal goal"},{"opt":"require a grounded Why prose paragraph per type","pros":"forces justification; exposes ungroundable advice; matches how reviewers explain decisions"}]
- **Rejected because:** Bullets+diff alone let the agent fabricate rationale-free advice
- **Discarded:** —

### D6: How the skill judges commonality
- **Decision:** Add a scenario-modeling step (who calls it, what environments, which patterns dominate) before judging commonality, and an explicit optimize-for-the-common-case principle
- **Options considered:** [{"opt":"label commonality directly","cons":"agent guesses; e.g. waffles on QuickGrid empty-prefix default"},{"opt":"first model real usage: callers, runtime environments, dominant vs rare patterns; then optimize for the common case","pros":"grounds the judgment; defends sensible defaults; serves rare cases via opt-in"}]
- **Rejected because:** Labeling commonality without modeling usage produced shallow/guessed judgments
- **Discarded:** —

### D7: Scenario modeling was in the skill but not surfaced in output
- **Decision:** Output format must show a Scenarios & environment line (environments + common-vs-rare) and carry it into per-type changes
- **Options considered:** [{"opt":"keep modeling internal","cons":"agent did not visibly apply it (missed cacheKey common-case friction, AdditionalAttributes type, static-SSR inertness on #66391)"},{"opt":"require the need assessment to SHOW a Scenarios & environment line and carry it into per-type review","pros":"forces the agent to apply common-case + environment reasoning"}]
- **Rejected because:** Internal-only modeling did not change outputs
- **Discarded:** —

### D8: Scenario model omitted input provenance
- **Decision:** Add input-provenance to the scenario model: reason about where inputs come from and what natural identifiers/metadata exist there
- **Options considered:** [{"opt":"model usage/environment only","cons":"agent judged required cacheKey as friction and proposed hashing the whole stream (expensive, defeats streaming, solves a non-problem)"},{"opt":"also model the nature/provenance of inputs and what is naturally available at the call site","pros":"a required key the caller already has (blob name/ETag/PK/path) is not real friction; make it easy to pass, not removed or synthesized"}]
- **Rejected because:** Modeling usage without input provenance led to expensive/incorrect default suggestions
- **Discarded:** —


</details>

> Note: draft — opening for feedback on content and on adopting repo-native skills under `.github/skills/`.

Issues.Title: Add author-analyzer-skill Copilot skill
Issues.Body:
## Summary

Adds a knowledge-driven Copilot **skill** under `.github/skills/author-analyzer-skill/` that teaches contributors (and agents) how to **author, review, and ship Roslyn analyzers and code fixes** in this repo.

The skill is intentionally lean (orchestrating SKILL.md in the standard token tier) with depth pushed into on-demand references:

| File | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `SKILL.md` | Where analyzers live, the "should this be an analyzer?" gate, the authoring workflow (starting with an explicit case analysis), canonical analyzer + code-fix shapes, the five rules that matter most, review checklist |
| `references/conventions.md` | Repo conventions: `ASP/MVC/API/BL` IDs, the `docs/list-of-diagnostics.md` registry (and why `DiagnosticDescriptors.cs` is the authoritative ID source), `Resources.resx` localization, help links, `WellKnownTypes`/`WellKnownTypeData` infra, project/packaging layout |
| `references/best-practices.md` | Roslyn best practices in a hybrid format: enforced `RSxxxx` rules as tight one-liners, judgment calls as self-contained Trigger/Decision/Why/Scope data points (performance, concurrency, correctness, diagnostic design, code fixes, anti-patterns) |
| `references/process.md` | Analyze the cases and document them, category, severity, implement, test/ship, with a checklist |
| `references/testing.md` | `CSharpAnalyzerVerifier`/`CSharpCodeFixVerifier`, `{|#0:...|}` markup, mandatory negative tests, boundary-pinning tests for documented non-goals |
| `eng/skill-evals/author-analyzer-skill/eval.yaml` | 6 held-out scenarios for skill-validator A/B evaluation (kept out of the shipped skill folder) |

Scope: analyzers, code fixes, conventions, and process. Source generators are out of scope.

## Case-analysis discipline

The authoring process leads with an explicit **case analysis**: before coding, partition the inputs into four buckets and design to that boundary.

- **Should fire** (true positives), each mirrored by a positive test.
- **Should not fire** (true negatives): look-alikes that are correct or intentional, each a negative test and a false-positive guard.
- **Detectable but too expensive**: patterns that need whole-program or deep data-flow analysis (e.g. following a call graph to an arbitrary depth), deliberately out of scope.
- **Undecidable from here**: facts that depend on a referenced assembly's *implementation* or another project's source. An analyzer reasons over the current compilation's source plus referenced **symbols/metadata**, never referenced **bodies**.

Design bar: be **precise where the answer is decidable** (in code that compiles, flag all of it with no false positives); make a **reasonable 80/20 effort** elsewhere and record the skipped cases as explicit non-goals. When deeper, costlier analysis would catch more, gate it behind a configurable **effort/strictness option** (`.editorconfig`/`AnalyzerConfigOptions`): conservative by default so live IDE editing stays fast, stricter in CI for deeper checks.

The `60_analyzer_proposal.md` template is reframed as the structure for the **follow-up PR description** (Usage scenarios = the fire/no-fire buckets; a new Non-goals & limitations section captures the out-of-scope and undecidable buckets), rather than a hard pre-build gate. A formal proposal is recommended only for opinionated or behavior-defining rules; routine analyzers are added and documented in the PR.

## How it was built (provenance)

Conventions were extracted from the repo's own analyzer projects, primarily the Framework set (`src/Framework/AspNetCoreAnalyzers`), corroborated against `src/Mvc/Mvc.Analyzers`, `src/Mvc/Mvc.Api.Analyzers`, and `src/Components/Analyzers`. Best practices were distilled from the `dotnet/roslyn` analyzers, the `Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.Analyzers` meta-analyzers (`RS1xxx`/`RS2xxx`), and official Microsoft Learn docs. Eval scenarios were informed by open `analyzer`-labeled issues.

Conventions are scoped to the **Framework set** as the model for new analyzers, with explicit "match the surrounding area" caveats where MVC/API/Components diverge (e.g. inline diagnostic strings, analyzers that reference Workspaces).

## Validation

Validated with the `dotnet/skills` **skill-validator**:
- **Static `check`:** passes; SKILL.md sits in the recommended token tier.
- **A/B `evaluate` (baseline vs. skill):** every scenario improved with the skill, with the largest gains where the unaided agent was weakest (fixing a false positive, diagnosing a non-working FixAll, authoring a new analyzer end-to-end).
- **Multi-model review** (GPT + Gemini) caught and corrected over-generalized claims (Framework-specific conventions, Workspaces usage, the registry not being exhaustive).

### Statistical A/B evaluation

skill-validator `evaluate`, `claude-sonnet-4.5` for both the agent runs and the judge, `--runs 5`, pairwise judging with position-swap mitigation, run against the committed skill. Quality is the judge's 1-5 score; baseline = agent with no skill, "with skill" = isolated average.

| Scenario | Baseline | With skill |
|----------|:-------:|:---------:|
| Author a new framework analyzer end-to-end | 2.2 | **3.8** |
| Review an analyzer with performance/correctness bugs | 4.0 | **5.0** |
| Diagnose a non-working FixAll in a code fix | 4.2 | **5.0** |
| Fix an analyzer false positive without regressing | 2.4 | **4.2** |
| Choose ID, project, and severity | 2.2 | **4.6** |
| Write analyzer unit tests with the repo's harness | 2.8 | **5.0** |

Overall improvement **+36.1%**, 95% CI [+33%, +50%], statistically significant. All 6 scenarios improved; **verdict: pass on every scenario**, no regressions. Three scenarios show high run-to-run variance (CV 47-100%), so a higher `--runs` would tighten the per-scenario intervals. The overfitting probe is informational and noisy at this run count (it swung across repeated runs); the eval rubrics score outcomes rather than the skill's wording, which is the real guard against overfitting.

## Notes

- Draft: opening for early feedback on content and on adopting repo-native skills under `.github/skills/`.
- The skill is documentation/markdown only; no product code or build changes.
- Eval scenarios live under `eng/skill-evals/`, outside the shipped skill folder, so the skill payload stays lean.

Issues.Title: Signals (Reactivity Components) for Blazor Interactive Render Modes
Issues.Body:
## Summary This proposal introduces a native-like reactive state management paradigm to Blazor using Signals (as in Angular Signals: https://angular.dev/guide/signals). It provides automatic dependency tracking to update components when data changes, eliminating the need to manually invoke `StateHasChanged()`. ## Motivation and goals * Blazor does not automatically trigger UI updates for state changes occurring outside of standard UI event handlers or input parameter updates (e.g., inside background threads, async tasks, or timer callbacks). Developers are forced to manually invoke StateHasChanged(), which leads to boilerplate, error-prone code, and maintenance overhead. * Provide a declarative mechanism inspired by modern frameworks (like Angular Signals) that automatically intercepts reads and triggers precise, automated component updates. ## In scope 1. Reactive primitives for "state management" (`Signal`, `Computed` and friends) 2. Track dependencies between those so we know when to re-render something and update the dependency graph ## Out of scope 1. I am not sure how SSR fits in here at this point in time. This proposal is strictly meant for Interactive Server/WASM. 2. Angular has many many more "signal" like things (`resource` or `httpResource` or even complete forms). While "nice", the initial batch of 3 to 5 (like `Effect` or `Input`) would be "good enough". ## Risks / unknowns Without deeply integrating into the Blazor Rendering Pipeline itself, we would need a wrapper component handling subscriptions etc. Therefore this could lead to a major driver of complexity inside the Blazor Renderer itself. ## Examples Here is an example taken from my [blogpost](https://steven-giesel.com/blogPost/495d87ca-61df-4c52-a253-8ba4abc186b7/signals-in-blazor) where I dabbled with the idea. It utilizes a reactive wrapper as boundary. Ideally we wouldn't have that in the final design. ```razor @page "/Signal" @implements IDisposable

Signal use

Elapsed time: @elapsedMilliseconds.Value milliseconds

Computed elapsed time: @elapsedSeconds.Value seconds

 @code { private readonly Signal elapsedMilliseconds = Signals.Signal(0); private Computed elapsedSeconds = null!; private Timer? timer; protected override void OnInitialized() { elapsedSeconds = Signals.Computed(() => elapsedMilliseconds.Value / 1000); timer = new Timer( _ => elapsedMilliseconds.Update(value => value + 1000), null, TimeSpan.Zero, TimeSpan.FromSeconds(1)); } public void Dispose() { timer?.Dispose(); } } ```

Issues.Title: The non-scaffolded razor page "Register" does not recognise Password.RequiredLength.
Issues.Body:
### Describe the bug
Setting password length through 
```
public void ConfigureServices(IServiceCollection services){
  ...
  services.Configure<IdentityOptions>(options =>
  {
    options.Password.RequiredLength = 1;
  });
  ...
```
does not change the non-scaffolded razor page as it always says "The Password must be at least 6 and at max 100 characters long." if inputed password is < 6 characters.

### To Reproduce
Steps to reproduce the behavior:
1. Create a new ASP.NET Core Web 2.1 application
2. Select "Web application (Model-View Controller)" 
3. Select "Change authentication"
3.1. Select "Individual User Account"
4. Select Ok to have the project scaffolded.
5. Open file Startup.cs
6. Add 
```
services.Configure<IdentityOptions>(options =>
{
  options.Password.RequiredLength = 1;
});
```
to method `ConfigureServices`.
7. Press F5 (or select your favourite start-debug method).
8. In the web browser that pops up
8.1. Select Register
8.2. Type "a@example.com" as user name (or select your favourite fast-to-type email addresss).
8.3. Type "a" as password (or any password shorter than 6 characters).
8.4. Select Register

### Expected behavior
The web page should have been updated with information about successful creation of user.

### Actual behaviour
The web page says "The Password must be at least 6 and at max 100 characters long."

### Additional context
I have a question at stack overflow https://stackoverflow.com/questions/53620708/how-do-i-set-password-options-in-aspnet-core-2-1 that explains the same issue if this is not clear enough.

This issue might be duplicate of #774.

Issues.Title: Remember me isPersistent understanding
Issues.Body:
This is more of a question but its not enough of a question that i could post it up on stack overflow without risking it just getting closed as primary opinion based or to broad.   So i am going to put it here in hopes of getting an answer.

The issue is the understanding of how remember me is supposed to work.    My problem is that its not doing what we would expect it to do.   

Management expects when a user logs in and clicks remember me that they will then not be required to type their login and password again for a month.  Whether or not they will still have to type in the 2fa code is up for debate.

What is actually happening is that i can close the browser reboot and come back for about an hour and 15 minutes and then i will be forced to type my password again.    This still happens even with a valid `.AspNetCore.Identity.Application` cookie set that is not due to expire for another month.

After digging around in the code i found that Remember me is actually isPersistent and it is defined as.

>Flag indicating whether the sign-in cookie should persist after the browser is closed.

Which brings you to:

There are two different types of Cookies:

- session cookies, held in memory, and which expire once the browser exits
- persistent cookies, which have a time-to-live, are persisted on disk, and are sent by the browser until their time-to-live has elapsed.

All this being said remember machine 2fa works as expected i am not prompted again for the 2fa and i have a cookie on my machine `Identity.TwoFactorRememberMe`.

So I guess my question is why are we not getting a `Identity.FactorRememberMe` or why isn't sign in respecting a long life time of the `AspNetCore.Identity.Application` cookie?

How is this supposed to work exactly?   I am following the code here even though we have normal MVC and not razor pages like you have in the example.  I am still just calling 

>var signInUserResult = await _signInManager.PasswordSignInAsync(userName, password, rememberMe, true);

Note: I am using Asp .net Identity in Identity server 4 application.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Closed issue "Remember me isPersistent understanding" (#2079)

Issues.Title: Should IdentityUser be in the EntityFramework namespace?
Issues.Body:
https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.aspnetcore.identity.entityframeworkcore.identityuser?view=aspnetcore-1.1

I'm looking through various implementations from the [readme](https://github.com/aspnet/Identity/blob/master/README.md) and I've noticed they all use `IdentityUser ` for their concrete type to pass to `UserManager`, or have had to craft a custom type to be able to have a contract between the database and the .NET AspNetIdentity implentation they've written (see the DynamoDB library).

I've had to do this too for the [Marten implementation I've written](https://github.com/yetanotherchris/Marten.AspNetIdentity), and the reason is, a `TUser` that your `UserStore` takes, should really be an interface - it will always have an email, most likely an Id of some sort, firstname, lastname and then a bag of properties.

Things become even more abstract when dealing with claims.

If everyone is using `IdentityUser` or their own custom `TUser` doesn't this defeat the purpose of AspnetIdentity - which is to be able to switch and change implementations easily? That isn't possible if your consumer is tightly coupled with a `TUser` implementation of the library.

### Example `TUser` implementations

- [MongoIdentityUser](https://github.com/tugberkugurlu/AspNetCore.Identity.MongoDB/blob/master/src/AspNetCore.Identity.MongoDB/MongoIdentityUser.cs)
- [DynamoIdentityUser](https://github.com/miltador/AspNetCore.Identity.DynamoDB/blob/master/src/AspNetCore.Identity.DynamoDB/DynamoIdentityUser.cs)
- [Redis uses `IdentityUser<string>`](https://github.com/aguacongas/Identity.Redis/blob/develop/src/Aguacongas.Identity.Redis/UserStore.cs)
Issues.RelatedDescription: Closed issue "Should IdentityUser be in the EntityFramework namespace?" (#2056)

Issues.Title: [Question] Passwords should not be of type String ?
Issues.Body:
My security team escalated to me that the `PasswordHasher` is vulnerable to heap inspection, as the password is of type `String`. 
It should be instead a byte array / span of bytes cleared at the end of the process, as it is done by any cryptographic operation in corefx. CC @bartonjs.
Same problem with any password related types (`IPasswordValidator`, and maybe also the `IUserPasswordStore`)
Issues.RelatedDescription: Closed issue "[Question] Passwords should not be of type String ?" (#2051)

interesting thing is i also have windows 10 on my machine & i am able to use web deploy on my local machine but it's not working with remote windows 10 machine.

is web deploy only meant for Windows servers not for other normal windows?

Thanks

Issues.Title: Feedback: ensure that .NET Core 1.0/1.1 tooling only supported in VS 2017 and won't be supported in VS 2019
Issues.Body:
Apologize if my feedback may sound controversial or may spark a debate.

I need to be sure that the problem mentioned on this [page](https://github.com/aspnet/Tooling/blob/master/known-issues-vs2017.md#to-create-aspnet-core-10--11-projects-install-visual-studios-net-core-10---11-developer-tools-for-web-component) about .NET Core 1.0/1.1 tooling is installed when creating new ASP.NET Core 1.0/1.1 project should not happen or should not be supported in VS 2019.

These are my main concerns:

1. VS 2019 is about to be released in around early 2019
2. ASP.NET Core 1.0/1.1 is not going to be supported anymore, based on [this page](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/master/microsoft-support.md).
3. Creating new ASP.NET Core 1.0/1.1 will always install the .NET Core 1.0/1.1 web tooling, and this is not necessary when previous reason has reached the unsupported period and it's quite near or might be the same time of VS 2019 release.
4. Based on the problem mentioned above, VS 2017 has no "built in" tooling when creating new ASP.NET Core 1.0/1.1 project, but the external tooling may help. Therefore for those that still need to maintain ASP.NET Core 1.0/1.1 projects, we could use VS 2017 as long as the VS 2017 is still in support lifecycle or in mainstream support lifecycle.

I welcome feedback 🙂
Issues.RelatedDescription: Closed issue "Feedback: ensure that .NET Core 1.0/1.1 tooling only supported in VS 2017 and won't be supported in VS 2019" (#1078)

Issues.Title: Support dotnet watch in launchSettings.json
Issues.Body:
I used to be able to use `dotnet watch` in Visual Studio by adding the following section to `launchSettings.json` and starting the project without debugging using CTRL + F5.

```
    "Kestrel (dotnet watch run)": {
      "commandName": "Executable",
      "executablePath": "C:\\Program Files\\dotnet\\dotnet.exe",
      "commandLineArgs": "watch run",
      "launchBrowser": true,
      "launchUrl": "https://localhost:5001/",
      "environmentVariables": {
        "ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT": "Development"
      }
    },
```

This used to work in ASP.NET Core 1.1 but no longer does. According to https://github.com/aspnet/DotNetTools/issues/404 the new format for ASP.NET Core 2.0 is:

```
   "Kestrel (dotnet watch run)": {
      "commandName": "Executable",
      "executablePath": "C:\\Program Files\\dotnet\\dotnet.exe",
      "commandLineArgs": "watch run",
      "workingDirectory": "C:\\Users\\namc\\source\\repos\\WebApplication2\\WebApplication2",
      "launchBrowser": true,
      "launchUrl": "https://localhost:5001/",
      "environmentVariables": {
        "ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT": "Development"
      }
    }
```

The problem with this is the hard coded `executablePath` which is Windows specific and the `workingDirectory` which is specific to wherever I did a Git clone on my local machine.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Closed issue "Support dotnet watch in launchSettings.json" (#1067)

Issues.Title: Nesting bug
Issues.Body:
The is a problem nesting embedded resources.  I was experimenting with nesting .cshtml files using the existing rules and did this:

Modal.cs (bootstrap modal web component)
   -> Modal.cs.cshtml (it's view file)

`Modal.cs.cshtml` is set to Embedded Resource, and clearly shows it, but is completed IGNORED by the build process (simply open the DLL in a text editor [such as Notepad++] and scroll to the bottom and look for text).  If I rename the file to "Modal.cshtml" instead it works perfectly fine!  :/  I don't think whether or not a file is embedded should have and role in excluding a file from being embedded so this seems like a bug to me.

Yes, I'm aware of upcoming nesting controls in v15.6, but I'm not able to install it yet, and regardless, this should work fine, so it should be looked into nonetheless.

Just to add: I'm using wild-cards to include the file.  If I explicitly add it I get this:

`Duplicate 'EmbeddedResource' items were included. ...`

So it's clearly found.  It's obviously something up with the build process not finding the nested file.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Closed issue "Nesting bug" (#1066)

Issues.Title: [Breaking change]: `OpenApiVersion` default change from `OpenApiSpecVersion.OpenApi3_1` to `OpenApiSpecVersion.OpenApi3_2`
Issues.Body:
### Description

The default OpenApi spec version is changing to 3.2 instead of 3.1.


### Version

.NET 11 Preview 6

### Previous behavior

`OpenApiOptions.OpenApiVersion` defaulted to `OpenApiSpecVersion.OpenApi3_1`

### New behavior

`OpenApiOptions.OpenApiVersion` defaults to `OpenApiSpecVersion.OpenApi3_2`

### Type of breaking change

- [ ] **Binary incompatible**: Existing binaries may encounter a breaking change in behavior, such as failure to load or execute, and if so, require recompilation.
- [ ] **Source incompatible**: When recompiled using the new SDK or component or to target the new runtime, existing source code may require source changes to compile successfully.
- [x] **Behavioral change**: Existing binaries may behave differently at run time.

### Reason for change

We aim to always use the latest version of the spec by default.

### Recommended action

Generally, no action is needed except if users encounter problems, like other toolings that don't support OpenApi 3.2.

If you're impacted, you can always configure the OpenApiOptions to use 3.1:

```csharp
builder.Services.AddOpenApi(options =>
{
    options.OpenApiVersion = OpenApiSpecVersion.OpenApi3_1;
});
```

### Affected APIs

- `OpenApiOptions.OpenApiVersion`

Issues.Title: ASP.NET Core 2.3 will reach end of support on April 13, 2027
Issues.Body:
# ASP.NET Core 2.3 will reach end of support on April 13, 2027

> Update: The ASP.NET Core 2.3 end of support date was updated from April 7, 2027 to April 13, 2027 to align with the Microsoft servicing release cycles.

Today we are announcing that ASP.NET Core 2.3 will reach **end of support on April 13, 2027**. After that date, Microsoft will no longer provide security patches, bug fixes, or technical support for ASP.NET Core 2.3.

This announcement provides the required 12 months of advance notice, as defined by the [Microsoft Support Lifecycle Policy](https://learn.microsoft.com/lifecycle/faq/general-lifecycle#what-is-the-lifecycle-policy-for-tools-) for products classified as "Tools."

## Current support policy

ASP.NET Core 2.3 packages (latest patched version only) are currently [supported](https://dotnet.microsoft.com/platform/support/policy/aspnet#dotnet-core) on .NET Framework, following the support cycle for those .NET Framework versions. After April 13, 2027, this support will end regardless of the .NET Framework version in use.

Note that the [ASP.NET Core 2.3 packages](https://dotnet.microsoft.com/en-us/platform/support/policy/aspnet/2.3-packages) include the Entity Framework 2.3 packages, which will end support on the same date.

## Impact of end of support

After ASP.NET Core 2.3 reaches end of support:

- No new security updates will be issued for ASP.NET Core 2.3.
- Continuing to use an unsupported version may expose your applications to security vulnerabilities.
- Technical support will no longer be available for ASP.NET Core 2.3.
- The ASP.NET Core 2.3 packages will be deprecated.

## Upgrade recommendation

We recommend upgrading to a [currently supported version of .NET](https://dotnet.microsoft.com/download/dotnet), such as .NET 10 (LTS).

To help with the upgrade process, we recommend using [GitHub Copilot modernization](https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/core/porting/github-copilot-app-modernization/overview), which provides AI-powered assistance to help you analyze, plan, and execute your migration to a modern .NET version.

## Resources

- [ASP.NET Core Support Policy](https://dotnet.microsoft.com/platform/support/policy/aspnet)
- [GitHub Copilot modernization](https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/core/porting/github-copilot-app-modernization/overview)
- [Migrate from ASP.NET to ASP.NET Core](https://learn.microsoft.com/aspnet/core/migration/proper-to-2x/)
- [Blog post: ASP.NET Core 2.3 end of support announcement](https://devblogs.microsoft.com/dotnet/aspnet-core-2-3-end-of-support/)

Issues.Title: Add automation to create docs tracking issues for breaking changes
Issues.Body:
Automates creation of documentation tracking issues in `dotnet/AspNetCore.Docs` when breaking change issues are opened or labeled in this repository.

## Implementation

**New workflow:** `.github/workflows/create-docs-issue.yaml`

- Triggers on `issues.opened` and `issues.labeled` events with "Breaking change" label
- Creates issue in docs repo with:
  - Title: `[Breaking Change] <original title>`
  - Body: Link to source issue, full context
  - Labels: `breaking-change`, `aspnet/Announcements`
- Posts comment on source issue with link to docs issue

## Duplicate Prevention

Multi-layer detection to handle race conditions and partial failures:
- Checks for existing bot comments in source issue
- Searches docs repo by title before creating
- Concurrency control keyed on issue number

## Error Handling

Separate try-catch blocks for each operation:
- Comment listing failures → proceed with search
- Search failures → proceed with creation (fail open to avoid missing docs)
- Comment posting failures → don't fail workflow (docs issue already created)
- Permission errors → explicit message about `DOCS_ISSUE_TOKEN` requirement

## Setup Required

Configure `DOCS_ISSUE_TOKEN` repository secret with `repo` scope for cross-repository issue creation. Falls back to `GITHUB_TOKEN` but will fail with clear error message.

## Security

Explicit minimal permissions: `issues: write`, `contents: read`

Issues.Title: [Breaking change]: Deprecating WebHostBuilder,  IWebHost, and WebHost
Issues.Body:
### Description

`WebHostBuilder` was replaced by `HostBuilder` ([generic host](https://learn.microsoft.com/aspnet/core/migration/22-to-30#hostbuilder-replaces-webhostbuilder)) in 3.0 and we also added [`WebApplicationBuilder`](https://learn.microsoft.com/aspnet/core/migration/50-to-60#new-hosting-model) in 6.0.

We are now marking `WebHostBuilder` as obsolete to encourage users to move on to the alternatives which is where future investments will occur, and we're also marking `IWebHost` and `WebHost` as deprecated to clean up other parts of the now obsolete web host story.

### Version

.NET 10 RC 1

### Previous behavior

```csharp
var hostBuilder = new WebHostBuilder()
    .UseContentRoot(Directory.GetCurrentDirectory())
    .UseStartup()
    .UseKestrel();
// Test code might use TestServer:
var testServer = new TestServer(hostBuilder);
```

### New behavior

Using `WebHostBuilder` will produce a compiler warning with diagnostic ID **ASPDEPR004**:
> warning ASPDEPR004: WebHostBuilder is deprecated in favor of HostBuilder and WebApplicationBuilder. For more information, visit https://aka.ms/aspnet/deprecate/004.

Using `IWebHost` or `WebHost` will produce a compiler warning with diagnostic ID **ASPDEPR008**:
> warning ASPDEPR008: WebHost is obsolete. Use HostBuilder or WebApplicationBuilder instead. For more information, visit https://aka.ms/aspnet/deprecate/008.

### Type of breaking change

- [ ] **Binary incompatible**: Existing binaries may encounter a breaking change in behavior, such as failure to load or execute, and if so, require recompilation.
- [x] **Source incompatible**: When recompiled using the new SDK or component or to target the new runtime, existing source code may require source changes to compile successfully.
- [ ] **Behavioral change**: Existing binaries may behave differently at run time.

### Reason for change

We recommend the use of `HostBuilder` and `WebApplication` going forward over `WebHostBuilder` because they have all the features of `WebHostBuilder` and will be the focus of future investment.

### Recommended action

See https://learn.microsoft.com/aspnet/core/fundamentals/host/generic-host or https://learn.microsoft.com/aspnet/core/fundamentals/minimal-apis/webapplication for more details on the different hosting models. As well as [HostBuilder replaces WebHostBuilder](https://learn.microsoft.com/aspnet/core/migration/22-to-30#hostbuilder-replaces-webhostbuilder) and [Introducing WebApplication](https://learn.microsoft.com/aspnet/core/migration/50-to-60#new-hosting-model) for migration guides.

Below is an example of converting `WebHostBuilder` to `HostBuilder`:

**Before**
```csharp
var hostBuilder = new WebHostBuilder()
    .UseContentRoot(Directory.GetCurrentDirectory())
    .UseStartup()
    .UseKestrel();
// Test code might use TestServer:
var testServer = new TestServer(hostBuilder);
```

**After**
```csharp
using var host = new HostBuilder()
    .ConfigureWebHost(webHostBuilder =>
    {
        webHostBuilder
            .UseTestServer() // If using TestServer
            .UseContentRoot(Directory.GetCurrentDirectory())
            .UseStartup()
            .UseKestrel();
    })
    .Build();
await host.StartAsync();

var testServer = host.GetTestServer();
```

Both Copilot and ChatGpt have given good results so far when asked to convert a WebHostBuilder example to use HostBuilder. Below is an example prompt:
<details>
<summary>Example AI Prompt</summary>

> Can you convert
>
> var hostBuilder = new WebHostBuilder()
>     .UseContentRoot(Directory.GetCurrentDirectory())
>     .UseStartup()
>     .UseKestrel();
> // Test code might use TestServer:
> var testServer = new TestServer(hostBuilder);
>
> to use HostBuilder.

</details>

### Affected APIs

`public class Microsoft.AspNetCore.Hosting.WebHostBuilder`
`public interface Microsoft.AspNetCore.Hosting.IWebHost`
`public static class Microsoft.AspNetCore.WebHost`

### Discussion

https://github.com/dotnet/aspnetcore/discussions/63480
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open issue "[Breaking change]: Deprecating WebHostBuilder,  IWebHost, and WebHost" (#526)

Issues.Title: [Breaking change]: Exception diagnostics are suppressed when `IExceptionHandler.TryHandleAsync` returns true
Issues.Body:
### Description

The [ASP.NET Core exception handler middleware](https://learn.microsoft.com/aspnet/core/fundamentals/error-handling#exception-handler-page), `UseExceptionHandler()`, is responsible for catching and processing unhandled exceptions from ASP.NET Core requests.

In .NET 8, we introduced [`IExceptionHandler`](https://learn.microsoft.com/aspnet/core/fundamentals/error-handling#iexceptionhandler). Implementations of `IExceptionHandler` are registered with dependency injection and invoked by the middleware. An implementation can return `true` from `IExceptionHandler.TryHandleAsync` to indicate that the exception has been handled. The middleware stops processing the exception once it has been handled.

In .NET 10, we're changing the default behavior so that the middleware no longer records diagnostics for exceptions handled by `IExceptionHandler`.

### Version

.NET 10 Preview 7

### Previous behavior

The exception handler middleware recorded diagnostics about exceptions handled by `IExceptionHandler`.

The exception diagnostics are:

- Logging `UnhandledException` to `ILogger`.
- Writing the `Microsoft.AspNetCore.Diagnostics.HandledException` event to `EventSource`.
- Adding the `error.type` tag to the `http.server.request.duration` metric.

### New behavior

If `IExceptionHandler.TryHandleAsync` returns `true`, then exception diagnostics are no longer recorded by default.

### Type of breaking change

- [ ] **Binary incompatible**: Existing binaries may encounter a breaking change in behavior, such as failure to load or execute, and if so, require recompilation.
- [ ] **Source incompatible**: When recompiled using the new SDK or component or to target the new runtime, existing source code may require source changes to compile successfully.
- [x] **Behavioral change**: Existing binaries may behave differently at run time.

### Reason for change

ASP.NET Core users have given feedback that the previous behavior was undesirable. Their `IExceptionHandler` implementation reported that the exception was handled, but the error handling middleware still recorded the error in the app's telemetry.

We are changing ASP.NET Core to follow the behavior expected by users by suppressing diagnostics when `IExceptionHandler` handles the exception. We're also adding configuration options to customize exception diagnostics behavior if needed.

### Recommended action

If you want handled exceptions to continue recording telemetry, you can use the new `ExceptionHandlerOptions.SuppressDiagnosticsCallback` option:

```cs
app.UseExceptionHandler(new ExceptionHandlerOptions
{
    SuppressDiagnosticsCallback = context => false;
});
```

The context passed to the callback includes information about the exception, the request, and whether the exception was handled. Returning `false` from the callback indicates that diagnostics shouldn't be suppressed. This restores the previous behavior.

### References

- Issue: https://github.com/dotnet/aspnetcore/issues/54554
- PR: https://github.com/dotnet/aspnetcore/pull/59074

### Affected APIs

- `UseExceptionHandler()`
- `IExceptionHandler`
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open issue "[Breaking change]: Exception diagnostics are suppressed when `IExceptionHandler.TryHandleAsync` returns true" (#524)

Issues.Title: [Breaking change]: Razor runtime compilation is obsolete
Issues.Body:
### Description

Razor runtime compilation is obsolete and is not recommended for production scenarios. For production scenarios, use the default build time compilation. For development scenarios, use Hot Reload instead.

### Version

.NET 10 Preview 7

### Previous behavior

Developers could use [Razor runtime compilation](https://learn.microsoft.com/aspnet/core/mvc/views/view-compilation) to recompile `.cshtml` files while the application is running. This is useful for development time so you don't need to restart the application for changes to take affect.

We're now obsoleting this and recommending developers make use of [Hot Reload](https://learn.microsoft.com/aspnet/core/test/hot-reload) which has the same effect for development time.

### New behavior

Using the APIs listed below will produce a compiler warning with diagnostic ID `ASPDEPR003`:
> warning ASPDEPR003: Razor runtime compilation is obsolete and is not recommended for production scenarios. For production scenarios, use the default build time compilation. For development scenarios, use Hot Reload instead. For more information, visit https://aka.ms/aspnet/deprecate/003.

### Type of breaking change

- [ ] **Binary incompatible**: Existing binaries may encounter a breaking change in behavior, such as failure to load or execute, and if so, require recompilation.
- [x] **Source incompatible**: When recompiled using the new SDK or component or to target the new runtime, existing source code may require source changes to compile successfully.
- [ ] **Behavioral change**: Existing binaries may behave differently at run time.

### Reason for change

Razor Runtime compilation has been replaced by Hot Reload which has been the recommended approach for a few years now. We're making it clearer that Razor Runtime compilation is not getting support for new features and should no longer be used.

### Recommended action

Remove calls to `.AddRazorRuntimeCompilation()` and use Hot Reload.

### Affected APIs

`Microsoft.AspNetCore.Mvc.ApplicationParts.AssemblyPartExtensions`
`Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection.RazorRuntimeCompilationMvcBuilderExtensions.AddRazorRuntimeCompilation`
`Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection.RazorRuntimeCompilationMvcCoreBuilderExtensions.AddRazorRuntimeCompilation`
`Microsoft.AspNetCore.Mvc.Razor.RuntimeCompilation.FileProviderRazorProjectItem`
`Microsoft.AspNetCore.Mvc.Razor.RuntimeCompilation.MvcRazorRuntimeCompilationOptions`
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open issue "[Breaking change]: Razor runtime compilation is obsolete" (#522)

Issues.Title: [Breaking change]: IncludeOpenAPIAnalyzers property and MVC API analyzers are deprecated
Issues.Body:
## Description

The `IncludeOpenAPIAnalyzers` MSBuild property and its associated MVC API analyzers are deprecated and will be removed in a future release. When `IncludeOpenAPIAnalyzers` is set to `true`, the build will now emit warning `ASPDEPR007`.

## Version

.NET 10 Preview 7

## Previous behavior

Previously, developers could set `<IncludeOpenAPIAnalyzers>true</IncludeOpenAPIAnalyzers>` in their Web SDK projects to enable MVC API analyzers without any warnings or deprecation notices.

```xml
<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk.Web">
  <PropertyGroup>
    <TargetFramework>net9.0</TargetFramework>
    <IncludeOpenAPIAnalyzers>true</IncludeOpenAPIAnalyzers>
  </PropertyGroup>
</Project>
```

The project would build successfully without any deprecation warnings.

## New behavior

Starting in .NET 10 Preview 7, when `IncludeOpenAPIAnalyzers` is set to `true`, the build will emit warning `ASPDEPR007`:

```
warning ASPDEPR007: The IncludeOpenAPIAnalyzers property and its associated MVC API analyzers are deprecated and will be removed in a future release.
```

The analyzers will continue to function, but developers will receive this deprecation warning during compilation.

## Type of breaking change

- [x] **Behavioral change**: Existing binaries may behave differently at run time.

## Reason for change

## Reason for change

The MVC API analyzers were originally introduced to help keep return types and attributes in sync for API controllers, ensuring consistency between method signatures and their corresponding OpenAPI documentation. These analyzers provided compile-time validation to catch mismatches between declared return types and the actual types returned by controller actions.

However, with the introduction of Minimal APIs and the `TypedResults` pattern, the .NET ecosystem has evolved toward a more type-safe approach for API development. `TypedResults` provides compile-time guarantees about response types without requiring additional analyzers, making the MVC API analyzers redundant for modern .NET applications. In .NET 10, `TypedResults` are supported in controller-based APIs.

**Previous approach with MVC API analyzers:**
```csharp
[HttpGet]
[ProducesResponseType<Product>(200)]
[ProducesResponseType(404)]
public async Task<ActionResult> GetProduct(int id)
{
    var product = await _productService.GetByIdAsync(id);
    if (product == null)
        return NotFound(); // Analyzer ensures this matches ProducesResponseType(404)
    
    return Ok(product); // Analyzer ensures this matches ProducesResponseType<Product>(200)
}
```

**Or with controller actions using TypedResults:**
```csharp
[HttpGet("{id}")]
public async Task<Results<Ok<Product>, NotFound>> GetProduct(int id)
{
    var product = await _productService.GetByIdAsync(id);
    return product == null 
        ? TypedResults.NotFound() 
        : TypedResults.Ok(product);
}
```

The `TypedResults` pattern eliminates the need for separate analyzers because the return type itself (`Results<Ok<Product>, NotFound>`) explicitly declares all possible response types at compile time. This approach is more maintainable, provides better IntelliSense support, and automatically generates accurate OpenAPI documentation without additional tooling.

As the .NET ecosystem continues to embrace `TypedResults` as the recommended pattern for API development, the MVC API analyzers have become obsolete and are being deprecated to streamline the development experience.

## Recommended action

Developers should:

1. **Remove the deprecated property**: Remove `<IncludeOpenAPIAnalyzers>true</IncludeOpenAPIAnalyzers>` from your project files to eliminate the warning.

2. **Suppress the warning temporarily**: If you need to continue using the deprecated analyzers temporarily, you can suppress the warning:

```xml
<PropertyGroup>
  <NoWarn>$(NoWarn);ASPDEPR007</NoWarn>
</PropertyGroup>
```

3. **Migrate to TypedResults**: Migrate to the `TypedResults` pattern documented above to ensure better consistency between application behavior and OpenAPI documentation.

## Affected APIs

- MSBuild property: `IncludeOpenAPIAnalyzers`
- Associated MVC API analyzers included when `IncludeOpenAPIAnalyzers` is `true`

Issues.Title: [Breaking change]: Deprecation of Microsoft.Extensions.ApiDescription.Client package
Issues.Body:
### Description

The **Microsoft.Extensions.ApiDescription.Client** NuGet package has been **deprecated** starting in **.NET 10 Preview 7**.  The package supplied MSBuild targets and CLI support (e.g., `dotnet openapi`, the `OpenApiReference` item group, and `OpenApiProjectReference` property) that generated OpenAPI-based client code during the build. Projects that reference the package will now receive a warning during build.

### Version

.NET 10 Preview 7

### Previous behavior

Projects could add `<PackageReference Include="Microsoft.Extensions.ApiDescription.Client" … />` and  `<OpenApiReference>` (or run `dotnet openapi`) to generate strongly-typed clients at build time.

### New behavior

 1. **Remove** any `<PackageReference Include="Microsoft.Extensions.ApiDescription.Client" … />` from your project.  
        2. Replace `<OpenApiReference>` items or `dotnet openapi` commands with generator-specific tooling:  
           * **NSwag** – use `npx nswag` or `dotnet tool run nswag` with an `.nswag` config file.  
           * **Kiota** – install with `dotnet tool install -g Microsoft.OpenApi.Kiota` and run `kiota generate`.  
           * **OpenAPI Generator** – invoke `openapi-generator-cli` via JAR or Docker.  
        3. Commit the generated client code or run generation in a custom pre-build step that does not rely on the removed package.
    validations:
      required: true

  - type: textarea

### Type of breaking change

- [ ] **Binary incompatible**: Existing binaries may encounter a breaking change in behavior, such as failure to load or execute, and if so, require recompilation.
- [x] **Source incompatible**: When recompiled using the new SDK or component or to target the new runtime, existing source code may require source changes to compile successfully.
- [ ] **Behavioral change**: Existing binaries may behave differently at run time.

### Reason for change

 * The package has seen minimal updates and maintenance since its introduction.  
* Its abstractions were tightly coupled to certain generators and did not scale well to others.
* Each generator now ships its own CLI/configuration experience, making the MSBuild middle-layer redundant.  
* Removing the package reduces maintenance burden and clarifies the recommended workflow for client generation.

### Recommended action

 1. **Remove** any `<PackageReference Include="Microsoft.Extensions.ApiDescription.Client" … />` from your project.  
2. Replace `<OpenApiReference>` items or `dotnet openapi` commands with generator-specific tooling:  
 * **NSwag** – use `npx nswag` or `dotnet tool run nswag` with an `.nswag` config file.  
 * **Kiota** – install with `dotnet tool install -g Microsoft.OpenApi.Kiota` and run `kiota generate`.  
  * **OpenAPI Generator** – invoke `openapi-generator-cli` via JAR or Docker. 

### Affected APIs

* NuGet package **Microsoft.Extensions.ApiDescription.Client**  
* MSBuild item **`OpenApiReference`** (all instances)  
* MSBuild property **`OpenApiProjectReference`**  
* CLI command **`dotnet openapi`**
